results.title: Value of asset token can be incorrect when usage of ETH/USD Chainlink oracle is needed
results.reasoning: Yes. The audit explicitly reports a decimals-normalization flaw in ChainlinkOracleConnector.getValue/getValueFromChainlinkFeed (Issue 11) where outputs are returned with the wrong scale (e.g., 8-decimal feed vs 6-decimal USDC), leading to systematic mispricing. The user’s vulnerability describes the same underlying problem manifested when routing via ETH/USD in NoyaValueOracle: the returned value ends up in ETH (18) decimals instead of USD (8). Both issues share the same code path (ChainlinkOracleConnector.getValueFromChainlinkFeed) and root cause (incorrect unit/decimals handling) and would be fixed by the same remedy of normalizing to the base token’s decimals (e.g., adjusting by the 10-decimal factor in the ETH/USD case). Despite different examples (USDC vs ETH/USD), they are the same vulnerability mechanism and location.

results.title: NoyaValueOracle.getValue returns an incorrect price when a multi-token route is used
results.reasoning: Yes. Item #10 in the audit content describes the exact same bug in NoyaValueOracle._getValue: the loop uses 'asset' instead of 'quotingToken' for multi-hop routing, causing incorrect pricing for routes with length > 1. It references the same function and code path and implies the same fix (use quotingToken). Fixing the issue as described in the report would also fix the vulnerability in the description.

results.title: executeWithdraw may be blocked if any of the users are blacklisted from the baseToken
results.reasoning: The provided audit report does not mention token blocklists/blacklisted addresses or the risk that AccountingManager.executeWithdraw can revert on baseToken.safeTransfer to a blacklisted receiver. None of the listed findings cover this specific root cause or code path (executeWithdraw’s transfer failing due to blacklist). Related queue/withdraw DoS issues in the report stem from different mechanisms (e.g., division-by-zero, pro‑rata burn mismatch) and would not be fixed by adding a blacklist check. Therefore, the specific vulnerability was not detected.

results.title: Loss of funds in PendleConnector.depositIntoMarket()
results.reasoning: The audit content does not mention PendleConnector.depositIntoMarket’s misuse of PendleMarketV3.skim() or the risk of losing surplus SY/PT to the Pendle treasury. The only Pendle-related findings are missing nonReentrant on swap functions and isMarketEmpty omitting Penpie staked LP checks. No item references depositIntoMarket, mint bounds (netSyDesired/netPtDesired), or removal of skim with pre-calculation via MarketMathCore.addLiquidity. Therefore, the specific vulnerability was not detected.

results.title: Incomplete TVL Calculation in AerodromeConnector::_getPositionTVL Function
results.reasoning: Yes. The audit explicitly reports that AerodromeConnector._getPositionTVL only counts the pool LP balance and omits the staked LP balance in the gauge (Issue 12), causing TVL underreporting. It cites the same function and code path and implies the same fix (include gauge balance via voter.gauges(pool) and gauge.balanceOf). Fixing it as suggested would resolve the described vulnerability.

results.title: PendleConnector incorrectly sends the redeemed PT tokens to the market
results.reasoning: The report does not mention PendleConnector.burnLP sending PT to the market (wrong receiver) nor the specific fix of changing the second burn() parameter to address(this). The Pendle-related findings (missing nonReentrant on swap functions and isMarketEmpty omitting Penpie balance) are different vulnerabilities in different code paths. Therefore, the specific burnLP PT-receiver bug was not detected.

results.title: A Vault can steal all funds from another Vault through the Registry’s flash loan contract due to insufficient access control in Connector.sendTokensToTrustedAddress()
results.reasoning: Yes. The audit explicitly reports the same cross‑vault privilege escalation via the global flashLoan address. Finding 33 (Cross‑Vault Privilege Escalation via Global FlashLoan Address Bypassing Per‑Vault Access Control) explains that registry.flashLoan() is granted onlyManager across all connectors and BalancerFlashLoan.receiveFlashLoan forwards arbitrary calls without validating they belong to the same vault, allowing a keeper of one vault to execute manager‑level actions on another vault’s connectors. This is the exact mechanism used in the described exploit (calling BaseConnector.sendTokensToTrustedAddress on another vault’s connector via BalancerFlashLoan to drain funds). Finding 13 (Flash Loan Address Grants Global Manager Access Across All Vaults and Connectors) and 38 (BalancerFlashLoan Allows Arbitrary External Calls via Unvalidated destinationConnector Array) reinforce the same root cause and code path. Fixing the audit’s issue (restricting flashLoan privileges and/or enforcing per‑vault validation in receiveFlashLoan / connector checks) would also fix the described theft via sendTokensToTrustedAddress.

results.title: PrismaConnector are not able to claim surplus collateral in recovery mode
results.reasoning: The provided audit findings do not mention Prisma’s surplus collateral recovery nor the absence of a PrismaConnector function to call TroveManager.claimCollateral(). The closest Prisma-related issues concern missing health-factor checks and registry updates on closeTrove, and general TVL underflow patterns, which are different root causes and code paths. Fixes for those would not add a claimCollateral function or enable surplus balance recovery. Therefore, the specific vulnerability was not detected.

results.title: AccountingManager::resetMiddle will not behave as expected
results.reasoning: Yes. The audit explicitly reports that AccountingManager.resetMiddle, when applied to the withdraw queue, does not reset currentWithdrawGroup.totalCBAmount, causing double-counting upon re-running calculateWithdrawShares. It references the same functions and code paths (resetMiddle and calculateWithdrawShares), the same mechanism (stale totalCBAmount accumulation), and the same fix (reset totalCBAmount to 0). Fixing it as suggested in the report would fix the described vulnerability.

results.title: SNXConnector.sol TVL calculation is incorrect
results.reasoning: Yes. The user’s issue states SNXConnector TVL is wrong for two reasons: (1) it doesn’t subtract debt from minted sUSD, and (2) it double-counts collateral by summing totalDeposited and totalAssigned. The audit report includes both exact problems as separate findings: #29 (“SNXv3Connector TVL Double-Counts Collateral by Adding totalDeposited and totalAssigned”) and #68 (“SNXv3Connector Ignores Minted sUSD Debt in TVL”). They reference the same code path (SNXConnector._getPositionTVL/mintOrBurnSUSD) and fixing them (use only totalDeposited and subtract SNX debt) would resolve the described vulnerability. Hence, it is detected.

results.title: Registry.sol#updateHoldingPosition remove position logic is incorrect: should use ownerConnector instead of calculatorConnector when calculating holdingPositionId
results.reasoning: Yes. The audit content explicitly flags the swap‑and‑pop bug in Registry.updateHoldingPosition where the key for the moved element is recomputed using calculatorConnector instead of the original owner/creator connector. Findings #3 and #25 describe that the original key is keccak256(abi.encode(msg.sender, _positionId, _data)) and the removal path wrongly uses holdingPositions[positionIndex].calculatorConnector, causing isPositionUsed mapping corruption. This is the same function, same mechanism, and the same specific fix (use the position’s owner connector rather than calculatorConnector) as in the vulnerability description. Although the audit’s impact discussion varies, it’s the same root flaw and fixing it would also fix the described issue.

results.title: BalancerConnector::_getPositionTVL is calculated incorrectly
results.reasoning: Yes. Audit item #64 explicitly targets BalancerConnector._getPositionTVL (lines 162–173) and its use of token weights in the valuation formula ((token value)/weight). The vulnerability description likewise states that dividing by weight is incorrect because getPoolTokens balances are already split by weights, leading to misvaluation. Both reference the same function and code path, the same flawed mechanism (misuse of weights in TVL computation), and are fixed by removing the weight-based extrapolation and using a fair LP pricing approach (sum of token values or pool.getRate). Although the audit emphasizes manipulability and the description emphasizes undervaluation, the root cause and fix are the same, so fixing the reported issue would fix the described one.

results.title: BalancerConnector has incorrect implementation of totalSupply, positionTVL and total TVL will be invalid
results.reasoning: The report includes a BalancerConnector issue (item 64) about TVL manipulation via spot reserves and weight amplification in _getPositionTVL, but it does not identify the specific flaw of using totalSupply instead of Balancer’s getActualSupply/totalActualSupply for BPT. The vulnerability described is about incorrect supply metric (pre-minted BPT), whereas the audit issue concerns manipulable inputs. These have different root causes and fixes; fixing the spot-reserve manipulation would not necessarily replace totalSupply with getActualSupply. Therefore, the specific vulnerability was not detected.

results.title: SiloConnector _getPositionTVL miscalculate the TVL position
results.reasoning: Yes. The audit explicitly reports the same flaw in the same function: SiloConnector._getPositionTVL values collateralToken, collateralOnlyToken, and debtToken share balances directly as if they were underlying amounts, instead of converting shares to underlying using Silo’s exchange rates. This matches the described issue (summing different share tokens and multiplying by asset price without conversion). The code path and impact (TVL/share price miscalculation) align, and the remediation (convert share balances to underlying then sum deposits and subtract debt) is the same.

results.title: It is possible to open insolvent position in Silo connector, due to missing check in borrow function
results.reasoning: Yes. The audit report explicitly identifies the same issue as the description: in SiloConnector.borrow(), there is no post-borrow solvency/health-factor check (minimumHealthFactor) via SolvencyV2.isSolvent, allowing creation of an insolvent position. This is item 42 in the audit content. It cites the same function and mechanism, the same impact (unsafe/insolvent borrowing and liquidation risk), and would be fixed by adding the same solvency check after borrowing. Therefore, fixing the report’s finding would fix the described vulnerability.

results.title: In Dolomite, when opening a borrow position, the holding position in the Registry will never be updated due to the removePosition flag being set to true
results.reasoning: Yes. The report’s issue #28 precisely matches the described vulnerability: in DolomiteConnector.openBorrowPosition (and also closeBorrowPosition) the call to registry.updateHoldingPosition uses removePosition=true, causing the position not to be added (positionIndex==0 short-circuits) and thus never tracked in the Registry. Same code path, same root cause (wrong removePosition flag), same impact (position not accounted, TVL/management issues), and same fix (set removePosition=false when opening/adding).

results.title: Numerous errors when calculating the TVL for the MorphoBlue connector
results.reasoning: The report flags only one of the three MorphoBlue TVL issues: borrowAmount is added instead of subtracted in _getPositionTVL (and its double-counting interaction with token registration on borrow). It does not mention the missing decimals normalization in convertCToL nor the missing _updateTokenInRegistry for the loanToken on withdraw. Since the vulnerability description comprises three distinct root causes, fixing the report’s issue would not fix the others. Therefore, it’s not the same vulnerability set.

results.title: Decreasing a position in PendleConnector will remove it even if there’s still a stake at Penpie
results.reasoning: Yes. Audit finding #59 describes the exact same issue: PendleConnector.decreasePosition uses isMarketEmpty(), which omits checking Penpie staked LP via pendleMarketDepositHelper.balance. This causes premature removal of the holding position while a Penpie stake still exists, excluding it from TVL. The proposed fix (add the Penpie balance check to isMarketEmpty) matches the vulnerability description. Same code path (decreasePosition -> isMarketEmpty), same root cause, same impact, same remediation.

results.title: Invalid calculation of position TVL in Pendle connector
results.reasoning: The described issue is a PendleConnector._getPositionTVL mispricing bug due to misinterpreting getLpToAssetRate/getPtToAssetRate (asset vs SY units). The audit content includes Pendle-related findings (missing nonReentrant on swaps and isMarketEmpty omitting Penpie-staked LP) but does not report the unit-mismatch/misvaluation in _getPositionTVL or misuse of getLpToAssetRate/getPtToAssetRate. Fixes for the reported Pendle issues would not address this TVL calculation error. Therefore, the specific vulnerability was not detected.

results.title: Invalid handling of holding positions in DolomiteConnector::transferBetweenAccounts
results.reasoning: The audit content does not mention DolomiteConnector::transferBetweenAccounts or the need to update registry holding positions after intra-owner account transfers. The only Dolomite-related item (#28) concerns openBorrowPosition passing removePosition=true, which is a different function, mechanism, and fix. No listed issue would be resolved by adding the proposed updateHoldingPosition calls in transferBetweenAccounts, and fixing #28 would not fix the described problem. Therefore, the specific vulnerability was not detected.
